cards.prompt: Profitability decline case — what are your top-level buckets and what equation anchors them?
cards.answer: Revenue drivers, Cost drivers, External factors. Anchor to: Profit = Revenue − Costs. Revenue = Price × Quantity; Costs = Fixed + Variable.

cards.prompt: Market entry case — list the four buckets in the order you'd present them.
cards.answer: 1. Is the market attractive? 2. Can we win? 3. Is it financially worth it? 4. How should we enter? (Bucket 4 is conditional on 1-3.)

cards.prompt: M&A case — what are the four core buckets, and what's the final test?
cards.answer: Standalone value of target, Synergies (revenue + cost), Integration risk/cost, Strategic fit. Final test: does total value created exceed price paid?

cards.prompt: New product launch — what four buckets would you propose?
cards.answer: Market demand, Competitive response, Internal capability/fit, Financial viability (unit economics + investment).

cards.prompt: Pricing case — what four reference points anchor your structure?
cards.answer: Cost-based floor, Value-based ceiling (willingness to pay), Competitive benchmark, Strategic objective (penetration vs. skim).

cards.prompt: Growth strategy — what's the organic vs. inorganic split, and what framework structures organic?
cards.answer: Organic: Ansoff matrix (existing/new products × existing/new customers — 4 quadrants). Inorganic: M&A and partnerships.

cards.prompt: Operations / cost reduction — what are the standard ways to bucket costs?
cards.answer: Process map → identify waste; or COGS vs. SG&A; or Fixed vs. Variable; or by function (R&D, ops, sales, admin).
cards.hint: Multiple valid cuts — pick by case context.

cards.prompt: NGO / social sector case (Diconsa-style) — what are the three buckets?
cards.answer: Benefits to stakeholders, Costs and risks, Feasibility and sustainability.

cards.prompt: What's the single most important question to ask before structuring ANY case?
cards.answer: What is the client trying to optimize for, and over what timeframe? (The objective drives the structure — always.)

cards.prompt: What's the MECE test, in two parts?
cards.answer: Mutually Exclusive: no item belongs in two buckets (no overlap). Collectively Exhaustive: no key driver falls outside all buckets (full coverage).

cards.prompt: Define NPV and when to use it.
cards.answer: Net Present Value = sum of discounted cash flows. Positive NPV = project creates value. Use to compare investments or evaluate go/no-go decisions.

cards.prompt: Define IRR and what you compare it against.
cards.answer: Internal Rate of Return = the discount rate at which NPV = 0. Compare to hurdle rate (typically 10-15% for corporates). Above hurdle = accept.

cards.prompt: Define payback period and why it's used.
cards.answer: Time required to recoup the initial investment. Quick gut check on risk; doesn't account for time value of money or post-payback returns.

cards.prompt: Breakeven formula?
cards.answer: Breakeven units = Fixed costs / Contribution margin per unit. Where contribution margin = price − variable cost per unit.

cards.prompt: Gross margin benchmarks: SaaS, retail, grocery, premium consumer goods?
cards.answer: SaaS 70-85%, Retail 25-40%, Grocery 20-25%, Premium consumer 50-70%.

cards.prompt: EBITDA margin benchmarks: tech vs. mature industrial?
cards.answer: Tech: 20-40%. Mature industrial: 10-20%.

cards.prompt: What is the Rule of 40 and what does it tell you?
cards.answer: Rule of 40 (SaaS): Growth rate (%) + EBITDA margin (%) ≥ 40 = healthy SaaS company. Captures the growth-vs-profitability tradeoff.

cards.prompt: Define burn multiple and the benchmark thresholds.
cards.answer: Burn multiple = Net burn / Net new ARR. <1 = great capital efficiency, 1-2 = okay, >2 = bad. Tells you how much you're burning per dollar of new ARR.

cards.prompt: SaaS — define ARR and MRR.
cards.answer: ARR = Annual Recurring Revenue (annualized run-rate of subscription revenue). MRR = Monthly Recurring Revenue. ARR = MRR × 12. Excludes one-time fees.

cards.prompt: SaaS — what's CAC, what's LTV, and what's the formula linking them?
cards.answer: CAC = Customer Acquisition Cost (sales & marketing spend / new customers acquired). LTV = Lifetime Value = ARPU × Gross Margin / Churn rate. Healthy LTV/CAC ratio ≥ 3:1.

cards.prompt: SaaS — what's CAC payback and the benchmark?
cards.answer: CAC Payback = CAC / (ARPU × Gross Margin), measured in months. Benchmark: <12 months excellent, 12-18 healthy, 18-24 concerning, >24 broken.

cards.prompt: SaaS — define gross retention vs. net retention (NRR / NDR).
cards.answer: Gross Revenue Retention = % of starting ARR retained, excluding expansion (caps at 100%). Net Revenue Retention (NRR/NDR) = includes upsells/expansion (can exceed 100%). Best-in-class NRR: 120%+.
cards.hint: One excludes expansion, one includes it.

cards.prompt: SaaS — define churn (logo vs. revenue) and benchmarks.
cards.answer: Logo churn = % of customers lost. Revenue churn = % of ARR lost. Benchmarks: SMB ~5% monthly is okay, mid-market ~1-2% monthly, enterprise <1% monthly (or <10% annual).

cards.prompt: SaaS — what is the magic number and what does it measure?
cards.answer: Magic Number = (Net new ARR × 4) / Sales & Marketing spend in prior quarter. Measures S&M efficiency. >1 = invest more, 0.5-1 = okay, <0.5 = pull back.

cards.prompt: SaaS — what's the difference between bookings, billings, and revenue?
cards.answer: Bookings = total value of contracts signed (forward-looking). Billings = invoiced amount in period. Revenue = recognized ratably over service period (GAAP). Bookings → Billings → Revenue.

cards.prompt: SaaS — what is PLG and why does it change the metrics that matter?
cards.answer: Product-Led Growth = users adopt the product (often free/freemium) before sales engages. Shifts focus to: activation rate, time-to-value, free-to-paid conversion, viral coefficient. CAC is lower but ARPU often lower too.

cards.prompt: Case prompt: 'Our client, a regional bank, is considering launching a new credit card. Should they?' — give 2-3 clarifying questions you'd ask first.
cards.answer: 1. What is the client optimizing for — profit, customer acquisition, cross-sell, deposit retention? 2. What's the timeframe and target (e.g., breakeven by year 3)? 3. Any constraints — capital, regulatory, target customer segment?

cards.prompt: Case prompt: 'Our client, a logistics company, has seen profits decline 15% over 2 years.' — give 2-3 clarifying questions.
cards.answer: 1. Is the decline revenue-side, cost-side, or both? 2. Is the decline uniform across business lines/geographies or concentrated? 3. What's happening with volume vs. price/mix? Any major external changes (fuel costs, competitor moves)?

cards.prompt: Case prompt: 'A PE firm is considering acquiring a mid-market SaaS company.' — give 2-3 clarifying questions.
cards.answer: 1. What's the investment thesis — growth, margin expansion, multiple arbitrage, platform play? 2. Hold period and target return (e.g., 3x in 5 years)? 3. Any operating constraints — willing to inject capital, change management, integrate?

cards.prompt: Case prompt: 'A non-profit wants to expand its tutoring program from 1 city to 10.' — give 2-3 clarifying questions.
cards.answer: 1. What's the success metric — students served, learning outcomes, cost per student? 2. What's the funding model and is it portable? 3. Are there operational constraints — staff, partnerships, target student demographic?

cards.prompt: Case prompt: 'A retailer is deciding whether to launch a private label brand.' — give 2-3 clarifying questions.
cards.answer: 1. What's the strategic goal — margin expansion, customer loyalty, defensive vs. national brands? 2. What categories are being considered, and what's the target margin uplift? 3. Capabilities — sourcing, manufacturing, brand-building experience?
cards.hint: Why private label, where, with what.

cards.prompt: Case prompt: 'A pharma company's blockbuster drug is going off-patent in 18 months.' — give 2-3 clarifying questions.
cards.answer: 1. What's the goal — defend revenue, harvest profits, reinvest in pipeline? 2. What % of total company revenue does this drug represent? 3. What's the pipeline status and any line extensions or follow-on indications available?

cards.prompt: Case prompt: 'An airline wants to know if it should add a new transatlantic route.' — give 2-3 clarifying questions.
cards.answer: 1. What's the financial target — load factor, yield, contribution margin? 2. Is this incremental capacity (new aircraft) or reallocation from another route? 3. Any strategic considerations — hub strategy, alliance partners, competitive response?

cards.prompt: What 5 clarifying questions should you have ready as 'always-ask' candidates for any case?
cards.answer: 1. What is the client optimizing for, over what timeframe? 2. What's the success metric or target? 3. What's the scope (geography, product, BU)? 4. What triggered this question / why now? 5. Any constraints (capital, regulatory, strategic preferences)?

cards.prompt: Why is asking clarifying questions a differentiator for McKinsey candidates?
cards.answer: It signals that you reason from objectives down, not from frameworks up. Strong candidates shape the problem before structuring; weak ones jump to a generic framework. Clarifying = client-mindset.

cards.prompt: What's the rule for HOW MANY clarifying questions to ask?
cards.answer: 2-3 tailored to the case — not all 5 generic ones. Pick the questions that most change your structure if answered differently. Asking too many wastes time; asking none means you're structuring blind.

cards.prompt: Retail — revenue per store decomposes into what three drivers?
cards.answer: Revenue per store = Traffic × Conversion rate × Average basket size. Total revenue = # stores × revenue per store.

cards.prompt: Airline — revenue equation in two equivalent forms?
cards.answer: Form 1: RPK (revenue passenger kilometers) × yield. Form 2: Load factor × Capacity (ASK) × Yield. Key costs: fuel, labor, aircraft (lease/depreciation), airport fees.

cards.prompt: Hotel — what is RevPAR and how is it calculated?
cards.answer: RevPAR = Revenue Per Available Room = Occupancy × ADR (Average Daily Rate). The single most important hotel KPI.

cards.prompt: Bank — define Net Interest Margin and what else drives bank revenue.
cards.answer: NIM = (Interest income − Interest expense) / earning assets. Plus: non-interest fee income (cards, advisory, transactions). Minus: opex and loan loss provisions.

cards.prompt: Insurance — what is the combined ratio and what does <100% mean?
cards.answer: Combined Ratio = (Claims + Expenses) / Premiums. <100% = underwriting profit. >100% = underwriting loss (insurer relies on investment income to be profitable).

cards.prompt: Manufacturing — what are the three key drivers of revenue?
cards.answer: Capacity utilization × Yield (% good output) × Price per unit. Costs split into raw materials, labor, and overhead.
cards.hint: Use it, get it right, sell it.

cards.prompt: Marketplace — what is GMV and how does revenue relate?
cards.answer: GMV = Gross Merchandise Value (total transaction value flowing through the platform). Revenue = GMV × Take rate. Both supply-side and demand-side liquidity must be tracked.

cards.prompt: Hospital — how does revenue decompose, and what's the largest cost line?
cards.answer: Revenue = Patient volume × Revenue per patient. Revenue per patient = Case mix × Reimbursement rate by payer (commercial > Medicare > Medicaid). Labor is largest cost (50-60%), then supplies and facilities.

cards.prompt: Why does payer mix matter so much for hospital margins?
cards.answer: Commercial insurance pays 100%, Medicare ~70-80% of commercial rates, Medicaid ~60-70%. A shift from commercial to Medicaid can swing margins multiple points even with flat volume. For-profit competitors often cherry-pick commercial patients.
cards.hint: Same patient, different payer = different revenue.

cards.prompt: Grocery vs. premium consumer goods — why does gross margin differ so dramatically?
cards.answer: Grocery: 20-25% gross margin, high volume / low ticket / low differentiation. Premium consumer (e.g., cosmetics, luxury): 50-70%, low volume / high ticket / high brand equity. Margin reflects pricing power, not just cost.

cards.prompt: Restaurant — typical cost structure breakdown?
cards.answer: Food cost ~28-32%, Labor ~28-32%, Rent/occupancy ~6-10%, Other (utilities, marketing, supplies) ~10-15%. Restaurant margins are thin: 5-10% EBITDA is healthy, 15%+ is excellent.

cards.prompt: Subscription consumer (e.g., streaming, gym) — what metrics mirror SaaS?
cards.answer: Same playbook: ARPU, churn, LTV, CAC. Plus: content cost amortization (streaming) or facility utilization (gym). Churn is everything — small monthly churn compounds to massive annual losses.
